title: ShareWELL Health Share
modulePurpose:
Major Medical Events, Self-Pay Process, and Pre-Existing Condition Limits

Section Purpose
This section teaches agents how to explain the health share portion of the MightyWELL bundle in simple, confident, and compliant language.
Every MightyWELL bundle is built using two core parts:
Third-party administered benefits for everyday healthcare
Health share participation for major medical events
Module 2 covered everyday healthcare.
This module covers major medical events.
Agents should leave this section understanding:
what the health share is
what the health share is used for
how the Unshared Amount works
how members practically use the health share
why there is no network limitation on the health share side
how to explain pre-existing condition phase-in clearly
how to avoid confusing routine care with major medical events
This module is one of the most important parts of the training because it directly affects trust, expectations, and compliance.

moduleSteps.title: Step 1 – Review Overview
moduleSteps.subtitle: Review Overview
moduleSteps.copy:
What the Health Share Is
A health share is a nonprofit community of members who agree to share eligible medical expenses with one another.
The health share used in MightyWELL bundles is a separate nonprofit organization.
MightyWELL is not the health share.
 MightyWELL is a marketing platform.
The health share operates independently and makes all sharing decisions according to its own member guidelines.

What the Health Share Is Used For
The health share is designed for major medical events.
That means things like:
hospitalizations
surgeries
emergency treatment
major illnesses
large unexpected medical events
This is not the part of the bundle used for normal doctor visits, prescriptions, checkups, or routine care.
Those everyday healthcare needs are typically handled through the third-party administered benefits covered in Module 2.
A very simple way to explain it:
Routine care is handled on the everyday healthcare side.
 Major medical events are handled through the health share.

The Unshared Amount (UA)
When a member enrolls, they choose an Unshared Amount, also called a UA.
This is the amount the member is responsible for before additional eligible expenses may be shared.
Common examples include:
$3,000 UA
$6,000 UA
A simple way to explain it:
The UA is the member’s portion before the health share begins sharing eligible larger expenses.

The Most Important Operational Concept
For major medical events handled through the health share:
There is no network requirement.
Members are not limited to PHCS, First Health, or any other network when using the health share for a major event.
That means they can pursue:
the provider they trust
the best surgeon
the best hospital
the best specialist available
A simple sales explanation:
For major medical events, you are not boxed into a network.
Another:
The health share gives you flexibility to pursue the best care available.

How Members Use the Health Share
This must be explained very simply.
When a member has a major medical event, they should tell the provider:
I am self-pay.
That is the starting point.
Then the member should begin the process by submitting the need through:
the app
email
phone call
The app is usually the easiest method.
A simple way to explain the process:
Tell them you are self-pay, get the bill or estimate, submit it, and let the health share guide the process.

Scheduled Procedures
If the event is scheduled, like:
a surgery
a planned procedure
a hospital admission
The member should notify the health share as early as possible.
This gives the health share time to:
review the case
confirm eligibility
help guide the member
work toward fair pricing
in some situations, arrange funds in advance
This is extremely important for scheduled care.
A simple explanation:
If it’s planned, start the process early.

Emergency or Unexpected Events
If it is an emergency:
Get care first
Gather the bills and records
Submit the need as soon as possible
A simple explanation:
In an emergency, get care first and handle the paperwork right after.

Important Payment Rule
Members should not rush to pay large final bills before the health share has reviewed the situation and provided guidance.
Why?
Because the health share may help:
review the expenses
confirm eligibility
negotiate rates
guide the member through the process
A simple explanation:
Do not write a big check too early. Start the process first.

Pre-Existing Condition Limits
This Is the Most Important Compliance Topic in This Module
Agents must understand this clearly and explain it clearly.
What the Pre-Existing Condition Limit Applies To
The pre-existing condition phase-in applies only to:
Major medical events handled through the health share
It applies to big events such as:
hospitalizations
surgeries
major illnesses
large unexpected medical needs
It does not apply the same way to routine care handled through the everyday healthcare side.
That means common day-to-day issues like:
high blood pressure
high cholesterol
diabetes
thyroid conditions
routine medications
are generally handled through the routine care / prescription side of the bundle, not through the major medical pre-existing limitation.
This is one of the most important distinctions in the entire training.
A simple line agents can use:
The pre-existing phase-in is for major medical events, not for everyday healthcare.
Another:
Think big events, not normal doctor visits.

What “Pre-Existing” Means
In simple terms, a condition is generally considered pre-existing if the member had symptoms, treatment, or medication for that condition before enrolling.
That does not mean the member cannot enroll.
It means that if that condition later turns into a major health event on the health share side, the sharing for that condition may be phased in over time.

How the Phase-In Works
For pre-existing conditions related to major medical events, sharing phases in over time.
A simple version agents should understand:
Year 1 – not eligible for sharing
Year 2 – up to $30,000
Year 3 – up to $60,000
Year 4 – up to $150,000
Year 5 and beyond – no phase-in limit
The point is simple:
It improves over time.
This phase-in is designed to protect the community from people joining only because they already know they are about to have a large medical event.

How to Explain It in Layman’s Terms
Use simple language like this:
If someone has a condition they were already dealing with before they joined, and that condition later turns into a big major medical event, the health share phases in how much it can share over time.
Or:
The pre-existing limitation is about big claims, not regular healthcare.
Or:
This is not about your normal prescriptions or routine doctor visits. This is about major medical events on the health share side.

What Agents Must Never Do
Agents must never say:
“Everything is covered right away no matter what”
“Pre-existing conditions do not matter”
“Your past health history has no effect”
“It works exactly like traditional insurance”
Agents must explain it honestly, simply, and carefully.

Why This Can Still Be Sold Confidently
The pre-existing phase-in is not a reason to panic.
It is simply a rule that applies to certain major medical events.
Most customers still care deeply about the fact that:
they have routine care access
they have preventive care
they have prescription support
they have a flexible major medical strategy
they are not boxed into a network
they can pursue quality care during serious events
The key is to explain it honestly.
Honesty builds trust.
 Trust builds sales.

moduleSteps.title: Step 2 – Review Materials
moduleSteps.subtitle: Review Materials
moduleSteps.copy:
Agents should open and review the Health Share Overview Guide.
This guide explains:
how the health share works
how the Unshared Amount works
how major medical events are processed
how pre-existing condition phase-in works
how to submit a need
Agents should keep the guide open while completing the quiz.

moduleSteps.title: Step 3 – Quiz Questions
moduleSteps.subtitle: Take Quiz
moduleSteps.copy:
Module 3 – Health Share
1. Which statement best describes the purpose of the health share?
A. It handles routine doctor visits
 B. It helps support members during major medical events
 C. It replaces all healthcare services
Correct Answer: B

2. True or False
Members must use a specific provider network when using the health share for a major medical event.
Correct Answer: False

3. When starting a health share need, the member should tell the provider:
A. I am in network
 B. I am self-pay
 C. Bill MightyWELL directly
Correct Answer: B

4. True or False
The easiest way to submit a health share need is usually through the app.
Correct Answer: True

5. True or False
Members should pay large final medical bills immediately before the health share has reviewed the need.
Correct Answer: False

6. Pre-existing condition phase-in applies primarily to:
A. Routine doctor visits
 B. Major medical events handled through the health share
 C. Normal prescription refills
Correct Answer: B

7. True or False
Pre-existing condition limitations are mainly about big medical events, not normal everyday healthcare.
Correct Answer: True

8. Which of the following is typically handled through the everyday healthcare side rather than the major medical pre-existing limitation?
A. High blood pressure medication
 B. Routine diabetes management
 C. High cholesterol treatment
 D. All of the above
Correct Answer: D

9. True or False
If a condition existed before enrollment, that automatically means the member cannot enroll.
Correct Answer: False

10. Which statement is the best simple explanation of pre-existing phase-in?
A. It means every past condition is permanently excluded
 B. It means major medical sharing for certain existing conditions may be limited at first and increase over time
 C. It means routine care cannot be used
Correct Answer: B

11. If a member has a planned surgery, what should they do?
A. Wait until after surgery to say anything
 B. Notify the health share as early as possible
 C. Only call the hospital billing department
Correct Answer: B
